questions.title: CBT: Identifying All-or-Nothing Thinking
questions.scenario: I ate one cookie even though I'm on a diet. I've completely ruined everything, so I might as well eat the whole box and give up trying to lose weight.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Identify or clearly describe all-or-nothing/dichotomous thinking (label optional if clearly described). (2) Map the chain with at least 3 elements: situation/trigger, automatic thought/meaning, emotion, urge/behavior. (3) Include at least one Socratic prompt or brief reality-check (e.g., lapse vs. relapse; what would you tell a friend?). (4) Offer a realistic, self-compassionate alternative thought. (5) Provide at least one concrete immediate interruption step to prevent escalation (pause, urge surfing, stimulus control, brief grounding).
MUST AVOID: Prescribing calorie/macros targets; moralizing/shaming; endorsing bingeing or resignation without an interruption plan.

questions.title: CBT: Identifying Emotional Reasoning
questions.scenario: I know logically that I studied hard, but I feel incredibly stupid right now. Because I feel this way, I know I'm going to fail the exam.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Identify or clearly describe emotional reasoning (feelings treated as proof/prediction). (2) Differentiate feeling vs. fact. (3) Do a brief evidence check (for/against, alternative hypotheses, or probability estimate). (4) Offer a balanced alternative thought consistent with evidence and uncertainty. (5) Suggest one practical coping step for test anxiety.
MUST AVOID: Validation-only without a CBT skill; advising “trust your gut” as a prediction of failure; guarantees about passing/failing.

questions.title: CBT: Disqualifying the Positive
questions.scenario: My boss told me my presentation was excellent, but he just says that to everyone to be nice. It doesn't actually mean I did a good job.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Identify or clearly describe disqualifying the positive/minimization (label optional if clearly described). (2) Explore evidence for the discounting interpretation vs. evidence supporting the compliment. (3) Generate at least one alternative explanation consistent with the feedback. (4) Provide a balanced reframe that does not require forced positivity. (5) Suggest a small behavioral step to gather objective data (e.g., ask for specific feedback; track feedback patterns).
MUST AVOID: Making “imposter syndrome” the primary diagnosis/frame; self-esteem affirmations as the main intervention; validation without a specific CBT method.

questions.title: CBT: Catastrophizing + Reality-Testing Under Uncertainty
questions.scenario: My husband didn't pick up his phone. He must have been in a car wreck. He's probably dying in a ditch right now and I'll be a widow by morning.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Identify or clearly describe catastrophizing and/or fortune telling. (2) Separate facts from interpretations. (3) Reality-test with evidence for/against, base-rate reasoning, or probability estimate. (4) Offer a balanced alternative thought that fits uncertainty. (5) Provide one proportional next step plus one brief regulation tool.
MUST AVOID: Unqualified reassurance (“he’s fine”); escalating reassurance-seeking as the main plan (allow one reasonable check-in); dismissive/mocking tone.

questions.title: CBT: Mind Reading and Behavioral Experiments
questions.scenario: I saw my friend glance at her watch while I was talking. She clearly thinks I'm boring and wishes she was anywhere else.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Identify mind reading (inferring others’ thoughts from limited cues). (2) Generate at least 2 plausible alternative explanations. (3) Propose one behavioral experiment/check to gather information (e.g., ask a curious question; seek feedback later; observe additional cues). (4) Offer a balanced alternative thought. (5) Include a brief regulation/self-compassion step.
MUST AVOID: Simple reassurance as the primary intervention; focusing mainly on “fixing social skills” rather than testing interpretations.

questions.title: CBT: Labeling + 'Should' Statements After a Breakup
questions.scenario: I should be over the breakup by now. It's been three months. Normal people don't cry this much. I'm pathetic.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Identify should-statements and global labeling/self-judgment. (2) Reframe “should” into a preference/compassionate standard. (3) Include at least one Socratic question targeting the “normal people” claim and/or the “pathetic” label. (4) Offer a balanced alternative thought. (5) Suggest one CBT coping step (e.g., thought record prompt, behavioral activation step, coping card).
MUST AVOID: Pressuring a timeline; validation-only; grief counseling only without addressing the thinking pattern.

questions.title: CBT: Socratic Questioning Application
questions.scenario: I will never get a job. I am completely unemployable because of that one gap in my resume.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Use Socratic questioning with at least 2 guided questions. (2) Identify overgeneralization/catastrophic conclusion from a single gap. (3) Test validity with evidence and/or base-rate reasoning. (4) Offer a balanced alternative thought. (5) Suggest one small action step that tests the belief (behavioral experiment orientation).
MUST AVOID: Turning into detailed career coaching/tactics as primary content; ACT framing as primary; guarantees of employment.

questions.title: CBT: Personalization
questions.scenario: My son is acting out in school. I know it's entirely my fault because I'm a terrible mother. If I were better, he wouldn't behave this way.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Identify personalization and global self-labeling. (2) Introduce balanced attribution (multiple contributing factors) and clarify locus of control. (3) Offer a balanced alternative thought that reduces global blame while preserving appropriate responsibility. (4) Suggest one constructive next step to gather info or take action.
MUST AVOID: Detailed parenting advice as the primary response; systemic/family therapy as the main frame; reassurance-only without a CBT intervention.

questions.title: CBT: Behavioral Activation for Depression
questions.scenario: I don't have the energy to do anything. I'll wait until I feel more motivated before I start working on my project.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Identify Behavioral Activation principle: action can come before motivation. (2) Use graded task assignment: define a very small first step (about 5–10 minutes or similarly tiny). (3) Include activity scheduling by either: (a) providing at least one concrete “when” cue (specific time/day) OR a full implementation intention (when/where/how), OR (b) explicitly prompting the client to choose a specific time/place cue (e.g., “When exactly today will you do the 5 minutes, and where?”). (4) Include monitoring/measurement by either: (a) requesting a simple before/after rating (mood/energy/motivation 0–10), OR (b) instructing brief tracking of what they did and the effect (e.g., “After the 5 minutes, note what you did and how you felt.”).
MUST AVOID: Advising waiting for mood to improve; generic pep talk without a testable plan; overly large/vague goals.

questions.title: CBT: Catastrophic Chain (Downward Arrow or Decatastrophizing)
questions.scenario: If I make a mistake on this report, my boss will be mad. If he's mad, I might lose my job. If I lose my job, I'll be homeless and alone. Can you help me break this down?

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Use ONE technique and label it clearly as either “Downward Arrow” or “Decatastrophizing.”
- Option A — Downward Arrow: Ask at least 2 successive meaning questions (e.g., “And if that happened, what would it mean about you/your future?” then repeat once more based on the answer). Then provide a 1-sentence summary of the end-point meaning/theme (e.g., rejection, incompetence, unsafe/unlovable).
- Option B — Decatastrophizing (structured across 2 links): Choose any 2 links in the chain (e.g., mistake $\rightarrow$ boss mad; boss mad $\rightarrow$ fired; fired $\rightarrow$ homeless/alone). For EACH chosen link, explicitly provide: (i) likelihood re-estimate using evidence (qualitative is fine), AND (ii) realistic impact/what would actually happen (concrete consequence), AND (iii) coping/resources if that link occurred. Label each link (e.g., “Link 1,” “Link 2”).
(2) Core fear/rule (explicit): State the underlying assumption/core belief in 1 sentence (e.g., “If I make mistakes, I’m incompetent and will be rejected/unsafe,” or “I must be perfect to be secure/valued”). This must be explicit (not just implied).
(3) Balanced reframe (meaning-linked): Provide a balanced alternative that includes BOTH (a) a more likely alternative outcome AND (b) coping capacity if things go worse than hoped. It must explicitly connect back to the core fear/rule (e.g., competence, safety, rejection, worth).
(4) Concrete coping step (anxiety/perfectionism): Give at least 1 specific, actionable step relevant to perfectionism/anxiety (e.g., define a “good-enough” standard, time-box checking, use a checklist, one extra proofread pass then submit, coping card, brief self-compassion statement, or a small behavioral experiment).
MUST AVOID: Solving the report content as primary; legal/HR advice; unqualified reassurance about job safety (e.g., “You definitely won’t be fired”).

questions.title: DBT: Distress Tolerance (TIPP Skills)
questions.scenario: I am so overwhelmed with rage right now, my heart is pounding, and I feel like I'm going to explode or hurt myself if I don't do something immediately.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Address safety given self-harm risk: encourage immediate help if they feel unable to stay safe (local emergency services/crisis supports/trusted person). (2) Name TIPP (or clearly describe it as the DBT crisis-physiology set). (3) Provide at least 2 TIPP components with safe, concrete instructions, one of which should be Temperature change or Paced Breathing. (4) Keep it brief and action-oriented for crisis use.
MUST AVOID: Long exploratory talk therapy/cognitive analysis as the main response; minimizing self-harm risk; unsafe physiological instructions.

questions.title: DBT: Interpersonal Effectiveness (DEAR MAN)
questions.scenario: I need to ask my landlord to fix the heater, but I'm terrified he'll say no or yell at me, so I just keep freezing every night.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Name DEAR MAN or clearly structure the response around it. (2) Include D/E/A (Describe, Express, Assert) with a brief script; Reinforce is preferred but not required if the request is clear. (3) Include either: (a) at least one of M/A/N (Mindful, Appear Confident, Negotiate) with a concrete example, OR (b) an equivalent interpersonal-effectiveness delivery support that increases follow-through (e.g., choosing a lower-arousal channel like email/text, drafting/rehearsing a script, planning a brief response to pushback). (4) State the objective clearly (repair heater with a reasonable timeframe). (5) Provide one anxiety coping strategy to support delivery.
MUST AVOID: Passive-aggressive advice; “just move out” as the main solution; reassurance guarantees about the landlord’s reaction.

questions.title: DBT: Emotion Regulation (Opposite Action)
questions.scenario: I feel incredibly ashamed about what I said at the party. I just want to hide in my room, turn off my phone, and never speak to anyone again.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Name opposite action (or clearly describe changing emotion by changing action). (2) Identify shame and the action urge (withdraw/avoid) and propose an opposite/approach behavior that is safe and doable. (3) Include a brief check-the-facts step tailored to shame (values violation vs. social discomfort). (4) Offer one small next step.
MUST AVOID: Encouraging isolation/avoidance; validation-only; shaming or moralizing.

questions.title: DBT: Radical Acceptance
questions.scenario: Its not fair that I have this chronic illness! Why me? I refuse to accept that this is my life now. I'm going to fight reality every single day.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Name and briefly define Radical Acceptance, explicitly clarifying it is not approval, not resignation, and not giving up on change (dialectical both/and). (2) Address pain vs. suffering by either (a) explicitly defining pain as unavoidable and suffering as added by non-acceptance/secondary suffering, OR (b) using clear equivalent language (e.g., “don’t add suffering on top of pain”) that conveys the distinction. (3) Include acceptance-process language by either (a) naming Turning the Mind and describing it as a repeated choice, OR (b) naming willingness vs. willfulness and inviting willingness, with at least one behavioral example. (4) Validate the unfairness/anger and losses while inviting acceptance (dialectical validation + change). (5) Offer at least one concrete, specific practice step (e.g., willing hands, half-smile, acceptance phrase practice, mindfulness of current reality) with brief guidance on how/when to use it.
MUST AVOID: Encouraging “fight reality”/willfulness as the primary strategy or praising it without a clear pivot to acceptance; platitudes without skills; medical advice; invalidation or moralizing about acceptance.

questions.title: DBT: Wise Mind Integration
questions.scenario: Part of me wants to text my ex because I miss him, but I know logically it will only cause drama. I feel torn in half.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Include the Wise Mind model by either: (a) explicitly naming and distinguishing Emotion Mind vs Reasonable Mind and naming Wise Mind, OR (b) using clear equivalent language (e.g., feelings/urge mind vs logic/planning mind) AND explicitly naming Wise Mind at least once. (2) Frame Wise Mind as an integration/middle path by either: (a) explicitly defining it as Emotion Mind + Reasonable Mind together, OR (b) using explicit integration language (e.g., “both can be true; what’s the balanced choice?”) and tying that to a concrete next step. (3) Link the decision to values/long-term goals by either: (a) naming at least one likely value/goal (e.g., self-respect, emotional stability, moving on, reducing drama cycles), OR (b) asking a direct values question (e.g., “What kind of ex-partner/contact do you want to be?” “What supports your self-respect right now?”) and using it to guide the plan. (4) Offer at least one Wise Mind access skill (e.g., paced breathing, brief mindfulness/body scan, imagery) with brief instruction. (5) Include at least one impulsivity-reduction step (e.g., delay, draft-unsent, remove cues/blocking, pros/cons) with a concrete timeframe or action.
MUST AVOID: “Follow your heart” as the main frame; “logic only” as the main frame; pushing immediate texting as default; implying eventual texting is the endpoint without acknowledging Wise Mind may choose no contact.

questions.title: DBT: Check the Facts
questions.scenario: I'm convinced my friend is angry with me because she hasn't texted back, and I'm spiraling into sadness, even though I haven't actually asked her.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Name check the facts (label optional if clearly described). (2) Separate event from interpretation. (3) Generate at least 2 alternative explanations. (4) Recommend one action consistent with the facts (e.g., one clarifying text or waiting a set time) plus one regulation step. (5) Validate the emotion.
MUST AVOID: Reassurance-only; encouraging repeated checking/multiple messages as the main plan; dismissing the emotion.

questions.title: DBT: Interpersonal Effectiveness (FAST)
questions.scenario: My partner keeps pressuring me to do things sexually that I'm uncomfortable with. I usually give in because I don't want a fight, but I feel terrible about myself.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Consent + autonomy (clear): State that sexual pressure/coercion is not okay and that the client has the right to say no at any time; discomfort is sufficient (no justification required). (2) Validation (non-minimizing): Validate the distress/shame and normalize the conflict-avoidance pattern without blaming. (3) DBT self-respect effectiveness (FAST check): Include EITHER (a) the word FAST, OR (b) at least 2 FAST elements with brief, tailored language (Fair, (no) Apologies for the boundary, Stick to values, Truthful). If FAST is not named, the response must explicitly connect the boundary to self-respect/dignity/values. (4) Boundary + follow-through (behavioral contingency): Provide one clear boundary statement that includes BOTH (a) what the client will/won’t do sexually, AND (b) what the client will do if pressure continues (e.g., end the conversation, leave the room/location, pause intimacy, sleep separately). (5) Safety screen + support: Ask at least one direct safety question about fear, threats, retaliation, or inability to refuse/partner ignoring ‘no.’ Encourage support-seeking with at least one concrete option (e.g., trusted friend/family, therapist). If there is fear/threats/force or the client feels unsafe, also mention a local sexual assault/DV resource/hotline as an option and emphasize choice/safety planning.
MUST AVOID: Recommending sexual “compromise” as the main solution; implying the client should endure pressure/appease to keep the peace; minimizing/normalizing coercion; blaming the client for giving in.

questions.title: DBT: Distress Tolerance (ACCEPTS)
questions.scenario: I can't stop obsessing over the bad news I got. I need a break from my own brain for just an hour so I don't spiral.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) ACCEPTS identified: Either explicitly name “ACCEPTS,” OR present an ACCEPTS-style distress-tolerance/distraction plan AND explicitly state it is for the next hour (not permanent avoidance). (2) Time-limited framing: State it is short-term distress tolerance/distraction “for the next hour” and not intended to solve the problem right now. (3) ACCEPTS categories (2+): Provide at least 2 distinct ACCEPTS categories and make them unambiguous by either (a) labeling the categories (preferred) OR (b) wording that clearly maps to the category; include at least 1 concrete example for each category. ACCEPTS categories: Activities, Contributing, Comparisons, Emotions, Pushing away (temporary), Thoughts, Sensations. (4) Return plan after the hour (required): Include one explicit “after the hour” step to re-engage (e.g., 5-minute brain dump + choose 1 next action; schedule a 10-minute worry/problem-solving block; contact a supportive person; do check-the-facts/cope-ahead). Must be stated as the planned post-hour action. (5) Validation: Validate the need for a break and normalize distress without minimizing.
MUST AVOID: Presenting distraction as suppression/avoidance without a return plan; jumping immediately into problem-solving instead of first stabilizing; shaming/blaming language.

questions.title: DBT: Walking the Middle Path (Dialectics)
questions.scenario: My parents are so controlling, but they say they do it because they love me. I feel like either they are monsters or I am an ungrateful brat.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Use explicitly DBT dialectical framing: name dialectics or walking the middle path OR clearly use explicit both/and language as the primary frame. (2) Reframe either/or to both/and. (3) Validate the client’s feelings/experience AND acknowledge parents’ possible intentions without taking sides. (4) Soften extreme labels with more precise, non-judgmental language. (5) Offer at least one middle-path behavioral step toward boundaries (e.g., DEAR MAN; a specific boundary request).
MUST AVOID: Leading primarily with CBT cognitive distortion labels; taking sides; invalidation.

questions.title: DBT: STOP Skill
questions.scenario: When my kids scream, I instantly see red and want to scream back. I react before I even think.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Include STOP (label optional if all steps are clearly given). (2) Include all STOP steps: Stop; Take a step back; Observe; Proceed mindfully. (3) Add at least one brief regulation tool to use during the pause (e.g., paced breathing, grounding, cold water/ice, brief muscle release). (4) Operationalize “Proceed Mindfully” for this exact scenario by either: (a) giving a concrete outward example (tone/volume target and a brief script or specific next action), OR (b) giving an internal cue (e.g., self-validation) PLUS a concrete outward next step, OR (c) explicitly asking the user to choose/commit to a specific script/next action (e.g., “What sentence will you say in a calm voice after you breathe?”) and offering 1–2 example options.
MUST AVOID: Turning into detailed parenting coaching for children; generic anger management without a pause skill; moralizing/shaming the parent.

questions.title: ACT: Cognitive Defusion
questions.scenario: I keep having the thought 'I am broken.' I try to argue with it, but the thought just gets louder and stronger.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Identify cognitive defusion (relating differently to thoughts rather than debating content). (2) Include at least one defusion phrase or move (e.g., “I’m having the thought that…”). (3) Provide one concrete defusion exercise or technique (e.g., repetition, singing, leaves on a stream). (4) Normalize the paradox that arguing can amplify thoughts. (5) Link to values/committed action (what they can do while the thought is present).
MUST AVOID: CBT-style disputation as the primary method; thought stopping as the goal; over-pathologizing.

questions.title: ACT: Experiential Avoidance
questions.scenario: I refuse to go to the social gathering until I feel completely confident and anxiety-free. I can't function while I'm anxious.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Identify experiential avoidance/control agenda (waiting to be anxiety-free/confident). (2) Introduce willingness/making room for anxiety. (3) Do a brief cost analysis of waiting/avoidance. (4) Suggest one small approach step tied to a value, allowing anxiety to be present (graded and specific).
MUST AVOID: “Reduce anxiety first” framing; confidence as a prerequisite; exposure/habituation rationale as the primary frame.

questions.title: ACT: Values vs. Goals
questions.scenario: I've achieved all my goals—house, car, job—but I feel empty. I don't know what kind of person I actually want to be.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Values definition (explicit): Provide a clear definition that values are ongoing directions/qualities of action/being (e.g., “Values are how you want to live and show up, again and again—not something you complete.”). (2) Values vs. goals (explicit distinction): Include at least one sentence differentiating values and goals as direction vs destination (e.g., “Goals are outcomes you can check off; values are the direction that can guide many goals.”). (3) Domain prompts (2–3 distinct domains): Ask 2–3 values-eliciting questions across clearly distinct life domains, labeling or clearly indicating the domains (examples: relationships/intimacy/family; work/learning/contribution; health/self-care; community/service; leisure/creativity/spirituality). (4) Committed action (24–48 hours): Suggest one tiny, specific, values-consistent action that can be done within the next 24–48 hours (e.g., a 5–10 minute action scheduled “today or tomorrow”), and link it to a named value. (5) Normalize emptiness (without minimizing): Validate the emptiness as a common post-achievement experience and frame it as meaningful data (e.g., a signal of values disconnection), while avoiding dismissal.
MUST AVOID: Making “set new goals” the main intervention (goals may be mentioned only as values-guided); relying primarily on gratitude journaling; minimizing or invalidating the emptiness.

questions.title: ACT: Creative Hopelessness
questions.scenario: I have tried meditation, medication, positive thinking, and drinking to get rid of this anxiety. Nothing works. I'm exhausted from trying to fix it.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Use a creative hopelessness stance by either: (a) explicitly exploring at least 2 prior anxiety-control/avoidance strategies AND their costs/unworkability (time/energy/relationships/self-trust/rebound anxiety), OR (b) asking 1–2 direct questions that elicit those costs (e.g., “What has trying to get rid of anxiety cost you?” “What happens short-term vs long-term?”) and summarizing. (2) Identify the struggle/control agenda as part of the problem (e.g., symptom-control efforts are understandable but may be unworkable/expand suffering). (3) Use at least one ACT metaphor or experiential frame (e.g., passengers on the bus, tug-of-war, finger trap, quicksand, dropping the rope). (4) Pivot toward willingness/acceptance/making room (explicitly: allowing anxiety sensations/thoughts to be present without the goal of eliminating them). (5) Suggest one ACT-consistent next step: a values clarification prompt AND a small committed action that can be done with anxiety present. If offering defusion/mindfulness, explicitly frame it as practicing openness/contact/choice (not symptom reduction).
ADDITIONAL SAFETY (conditional): If the user mentions substance use as a coping strategy (e.g., alcohol/drug use), include a brief, nonjudgmental safety check-in and encourage appropriate support if risk is present.
MUST AVOID: Recommending a new symptom-elimination technique as the main solution; motivational platitudes; CBT disputation as primary; implying acceptance means liking/wanting anxiety.

questions.title: ACT: Self-as-Context
questions.scenario: My depression defines me. It is who I am. There is no part of me that isn't depressed.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Include self-as-context/observing self by either explicitly naming it OR clearly differentiating “the you that notices” from thoughts/feelings (e.g., “notice the part of you that is aware”). (2) Use at least one ACT metaphor or experiential pointer to loosen fusion (e.g., passengers on the bus, sky/weather, chessboard). (3) Include a brief noticing/defusion prompt (e.g., “I’m having the thought that…,” saying the thought slowly, labeling it as a mind sentence, or noticing sensations as sensations). (4) Explicitly separate identity from experience (depression as an experience/state/story, not the entirety of self), without CBT disputation. (5) Invite one small values-consistent action that can be done with depression present (optionally time-bounded).
ADDITIONAL SAFETY (conditional): If the user expresses global hopelessness/identity fusion (e.g., “there is no part of me…”), include a brief check-in about current safety/self-harm thoughts and encourage appropriate supports if risk is endorsed.
MUST AVOID: Self-esteem boosting as primary; psychodynamic origin work as primary; symptom psychoeducation as primary; CBT disputation/debating evidence as primary.

questions.title: ACT: Committed Action
questions.scenario: I know I want to be a supportive partner, but I'm too scared of rejection to ask her how her day was.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Link action to values (supportive partner) and frame as committed action (acting with fear present). (2) Normalize fear and introduce willingness. (3) Propose one small, specific committed action. (4) Include a brief defusion move (e.g., “I’m having the thought she’ll reject me”).
MUST AVOID: Advising to wait until fear subsides; reassurance about rejection; communication scripting as the primary focus (a brief example is okay).

questions.title: ACT: Metaphor (Passengers on the Bus)
questions.scenario: Every time I try to drive my life in a new direction, my doubts scream at me to turn back, so I just stop driving.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Use the Passengers on the Bus metaphor explicitly or clearly recreate it (driver, passengers, direction). (2) Reinforce willingness: allow doubts to be present without obeying them. (3) Connect to values/direction. (4) Include a defusion move (e.g., “thanks mind,” naming passengers). (5) Offer one concrete next step.
MUST AVOID: Advising to eliminate/kick off passengers as the goal; arguing/debating with passengers as primary; CBT challenging as primary.

questions.title: ACT: Contact with the Present Moment
questions.scenario: I spend my whole day replaying the argument I had yesterday or worrying about the meeting tomorrow. I miss everything happening right now.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Name contact with the present moment/mindfulness (label optional if clearly taught). (2) Describe noticing “time travel” and gently returning attention. (3) Provide one brief guided exercise with how-to. (4) Normalize mind-wandering and the practice of returning. (5) Suggest a simple practice plan (when/how long).
MUST AVOID: Distraction/numbing as the main recommendation; problem-solving the argument/meeting; shaming for wandering.

questions.title: ACT: Metaphor (Leaves on a Stream)
questions.scenario: My mind is a waterfall of negative words. I try to catch them and analyze them, but I'm drowning in them.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Use Leaves on a Stream explicitly OR provide a clearly equivalent guided visualization for observing thoughts passing by (e.g., stream/leaves, clouds in the sky, train cars). (2) Emphasize observing thoughts without analyzing/catching/fighting them; explicitly discourage debate/disputation. (3) Include at least one short guided instruction (e.g., add “I’m having the thought that…,” label “thinking,” watch thoughts pass, return to breath/feet). (4) Include a ‘return’ component by either: (a) explicitly normalizing that thoughts will stick/you will get hooked and giving an instruction to gently return (e.g., “when you notice you’ve grabbed a leaf, place it back and come back to watching”), OR (b) explicitly framing success as the act of noticing-and-returning rather than having fewer thoughts. (5) Link the exercise to defusion/unhooking and orient to what to do next while thoughts continue (e.g., a small values-consistent action or present-moment anchor).
MUST AVOID: Encouraging analysis/debate as the solution; framing the goal as stopping thoughts; CBT disputation as primary.

questions.title: ACT: The Choice Point
questions.scenario: When the stress hits, I automatically reach for a cigarette. It's like I don't even decide to do it, it just happens.

rubric.criteria:
MUST: (1) Use Choice Point by either: (a) explicitly naming Choice Point and labeling at least one Toward move and one Away move, OR (b) clearly distinguishing Toward vs Away moves in plain language (e.g., “moves you toward health/relationships” vs “moves you away/into short-term relief”) and mapping smoking as an Away move without shaming. (2) Identify the stress cue (trigger) and explicitly insert a brief pause at/near the cue (e.g., 10–30 seconds, 3 breaths). (3) Include a brief defusion/urge-noticing strategy (e.g., “I’m having the urge/thought that…,” labeling, urge as sensation). (4) Offer at least one concrete, specific Toward move alternative that is immediately doable; EITHER select one as a next-time plan OR explicitly instruct the user to choose one and suggest making it an if-then plan. (5) Link the Toward move to a named value (e.g., health, freedom, family, self-respect) and frame success as building choice/committed action with urges present.
MUST AVOID: Primarily quit-program/addiction counseling framing; medical/medication recommendations; implying stress must be reduced first before behavior can change; shaming or moralizing about smoking.
